California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
December 2■′ ■990
Mr. Robert M. Hertzberg
Margo■is′  Hertzberg & Morin
3550 Wilshire B■
vd.′  Suite

ce
Our File No. A-90-74■
Dear Mr. Hertzberg:
You haVe requested advice on behalf of Los Angeles City

imits of
OUESTIONS
L. Does section 85305, which sets forth the franework of
contribution Iimitations for special elections, apply to special
elections only after December 4, L99o, the date of the Commission
meeting discussing the section?
2. Does section 85305 appty to special- elections only after
November 21 , L99o, the date that commission staff informed
candidates in the Los Angeles county Board of Supervisors'
eLection scheduled for January 22, L99]. , that the contribution
linits applied to special elections?
CONCLUS IONS
L & 2. No. Neither the decision of the federal distri.ct
court in Service Emplovees International- Union et aI. v. Fair
Political Practices Cornmission, Case No. CIVS 89-433 LKK (trSEIUrt),
nor any action by the commission or its staff rendered Section

Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

85305 at any tine inapplicable to speciaf etections.2 Thus
Section 85305 has applied to special- elections since its
enactment.
FACTS
Councilwornan Motina is a candidate in a special election3 for
a seat on the Los Angeles county Board of Supervj-sors. The
election is scheduled for January 22, 199L.
on November 2L, 1990, the Mo]ina for Supervisor campaign (the
t,campa j,gn,r) received a letter f rorn Cornrnission staf f inf onning all
candidafes about the special election contribution linits of
Section 85305. Before that date the canpaign accepted a number of
contributions in excess of the Limits of Secti-on 85305. The
carnpaign claims that conmj,ssion staff, on more than one occasion,
aavised the canpaign over the telephone that the recent federal
district 
court hecision in SEIU had invalidated al,1 contribution
Iimits applicable to the Los Angeles county supervisor's election'
ANALYSI S
A. Procedural Backsround
The voters enacted Section 85305 in 1988 as part of the
political 
canpaign reform initiative, 
Proposition 73. Section
-es:os sets foith a franework of contribution lirnits on candidates,
potitical 
comrnittees, and broad based political 
connittees during
a speciat elect j-on cYcle.
on September 25, :-990, the federaL distrj-ct court, Eastern
District oi c"Iifort ia, declared unconstitutiona 1 contribution
Iimj,ts enacted by Proposition 73 and measured in reLation to the
fiscal year. 
(SEIU, conclusions of La\'/, filed Septenber 25,
1990. ) The court ordered:
1. AII contribution linitations 
measured on a
fiscal vear basis violate the provisions of the
2 Yo,, have requested review of these conclusions by the fu11
Comrnission. Because of the exceptional nature of your guestions,
pending review by the Commission at its January 9, 7990; rneeting,
the ttotin" for Supervisor Campaign may continue to hofd the excess
contributj.ons which were collected prior to December 4, L99o'
3 thi= Ietter presunes that the election i.s a rrspeciaL election.'l
whether the election is j,ndeed a rrspecial election[ is currently
being litigated before the California Superior court, county of
Los Angeles, in Rubalcava, et aI. v. l'leissburd. et al., No.
BSoo4749. In the event the courts decide that the supervisor's
election is not a "special election,rt then your questions become
noot, and Section 85305 will not apply to this board of
supervisors' election.


Our File No. A-90-74■
Page 3
First Amendnent to the Constitution of the United
States and are hereby declared unenforceable;
accordingly, enforcement of all such provisions are
PERMANENiLY RESTRAINED ; 4
conclusions of La$r, p. 32,
ernphasis added.
The district court invalidated no other contribution lirnits.
on December 4, 7g9O I the commission conf irrned staff's advice
that the contribution lirnits of Proposition 73 rernained in effect
as they related to special elections.
B. Application of Section 85305 to the Molina Camoaiqn
Your letter makes rra formal inquiry as to whether the
comrnission's December 4th action is retroactive frorn the date of
such action. Alternatively, inquiry is made as to whether, at a
minirnum, the retroactive effect of the Comrnission's action should
be from November zlt Lggo forward since that was the date that a1I
of the campaigns received notification of the commission's
intended action. "
Your inquiry sonewhat mischaracteri zes the effect of the
district 
couit's decision upon Section 85305 and the Comrnission's
actions since the decision, I'Retroactivity" irnplies that there
was some change in the law. That is not the case here. First,
Section 85305 has been in effect since the enactment of
Proposition 73 in 1988. As evident from the language of the
district court's order cited above, the distrj-ct court invalidated
only those contri,bution limits based on a fiscal year basis.
Beciuse section 85305 is based on a special election cyc1e, it is
unaffected.
Second, the conmission's action on Decenber 4 and the staff's
action on November 2L, L99O, merely affirmed the current state of
the lalr--that contribution linits 
rernained in effect for special
elections. 
The connission's action on December 4 nerely directed
commission staff to continue to give such advice.
This is consj-stent with the Commission's povrers under the
Iaw. Absent a clear declaration by an appellate court, neither
the comrnission nor the commission staff has the legal authority to
4 on September 23, 1990, the SEIU court stayed its order as it
reLated to candidates for the California Legislature until rrthere
is a final judgnent that the campaign contribution limitations of
Proposition 68 are not effective.rr Proposition 68, rrhich applied
contribution Limits in Iegislative races, was made inoperative by
Proposition 73. Therefore, aII of Proposition 73,s contribution
fimits remain in effect for legislative candidates until a f inal-
judgrnent issues which invalidates Propositi-on 68.


Our Fi■e No. A-90-74■
Page 4
declare a provision of the act unenforceable or refuse to enforce
a statute on constitutional grounds. (ca1. constitution, Article
III, 
Section 3.5(a), (b).)
Moreover, Cornrnission staff has no record of anyone
identifying hinself or herself with the l'1o1ina for Supervisor
carapaign and calling to request advice on the application of
contribution limits to special el-ections. we do have a record of
one caller on october 4, L99o, requesting information about the
general applicability of contribution linj,ts to a Los Angeles
-ounty Board of Supervisor/s election. 
However, that calIer did
not disclose that the question concerned a special election or
that he was calling on behalf of any candidate. Even if
Commission staff had given the advice that the carnpaign clains,
such oral advice, as informal advice, cannot imnunize actions
contrary to the Act. 
(Section 83114(b); Regulation 18329, copy
encl,osed . )
Finally, any stay in the effect of Section 85305 \"rouId allot^/
the MoIlna ior supervisor campaign to keep contributions in excess
of the lega1 limits and be contrary to the advice qiven to the
public. 
as early as the Commission's october 2, L99o, meeting,
commission staff has been takj-ng the position that SEIU did not
affect the lirnits on special elections. 
And prior to the letter
of November 27, L99o, and the Cornmission neeting of Decenber 4,
1990, conmission staff advised other candidates in the Board of
Supervisors' election that Section 85305 rernained in effect and
thlt contribution Iirnits stiIl 
appJ-ied to special- elections.
Staying the effect of Section 85305 would effectively put the
Mol-ina campaign's oPponents, who followed the Iaw and did not
collect excess contributions, at a clear dj.sadvantage.
In sum, contribution limits as applied to special elections
have rernained in effect si-nce the enactment of Proposition 73, and
neither the actions of the federal district 
court nor the
commission has stayed the effect of Section 85305 upon the Los
Angeles county Board of supervisor/s election or the Mo]ina
campaign.
If you have any further questj,ons regarding this natter
pLease contact me at (916) 322-5901.
S incerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting ceneral counsel
